user_request: Draft a suit for partition and separate possession of ancestral property. Plaintiff entitled to 1/3rd share. Defendant refusing amicable partition. Include genealogy table and schedule of property. Seek preliminary decree. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks suit for partition and separate possession of ancestral property, claiming a 1/3rd share. Defendant is refusing amicable partition, necessitating legal action for a preliminary decree.

missing_fields: Valuation of Property for Court Fee

Plaintiff and Defendant are legal heirs/coparceners

6. Partition decree. In a suit for partition, there are normally two decrees: (i) a preliminary decree; and (ii) a final 
decree. There might be a preliminary decree or even more than one preliminary decree, which would determine the 
share of the party, but which would not divide the properties deliverable to the co-sharer. Another stage is that of 
passing of final decree after inquiry is held, as per the directions contained in the preliminary decree. The shares of 
the party are determined first at the stage of preliminary decree, and the actual partition is effected as per the final 
decree.31
When the parties to a suit filed a compromise petition with the plan partitioning their respective shares by metes and 
bounds, no further inquiry is needed unless it is found to be not genuine. In such a case passing a preliminary 
decree is not necessary and the court can directly pass a final decree in terms of the compromise.32
Generally speaking, a final decree cannot amend or go behind the preliminary decree on a matter decided by the 
preliminary decree. 33 However, where some of the parties after the passing of the preliminary decree give up

final decree in a suit. On that, judicial opinion was divided; some decisions took the view that there could be 
only one preliminary decree and one final decree,76 while others held that there was nothing in the Code of Civil 
Procedure prohibiting the passing of more than one preliminary or final decree.77
In accordance with this view, where the plaintiff was found to be entitled to contribution but the amount was left 
to be determined later on, it was held that the order was a preliminary decree.78

sections.instruction: Draft the suit title line based on the nature of the suit, relief sought, and any monetary amount involved. Use standard Indian court pleading format. If this is a property suit use 'SUIT FOR PARTITION AND SEPARATE POSSESSION' style. If recovery suit use 'SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/-' style. Adapt to the actual suit type from the classified doc_type and intake facts.

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff(s) and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, address. Use competency averment (sui juris / competent to sue). If any detail is missing, use {{PLACEHOLDER}}. Adapt party labels to the suit type (e.g., Petitioner/Respondent for writ petitions).

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction based on the facts. For territorial jurisdiction, state the SPECIFIC factual basis: where the defendant resides, where property is situated, where the cause of action arose, or where the transaction occurred. For suits involving immovable property, jurisdiction is based on where the property is situated (Section 16 CPC). For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the suit valuation and that it falls within the court's monetary limits.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Adapt the narrative to the actual nature of the suit: for property suits describe ownership chain and possession history, for recovery suits describe the transaction and default, for family matters describe relationships and events. Reference documents as Annexure labels. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

must_include.description: At least one date or date placeholder

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section citing relevant statutory provisions and legal principles. For EACH provision: (1) State the legal rule it creates, (2) State what conditions trigger it, (3) Show how the facts satisfy those conditions. Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list or those naturally applicable to the suit type. If applicable, include alternative pleas using 'In the alternative and without prejudice...' framing. Include defensive pleading to pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments.

must_include.description: At least one statutory provision cited

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose. Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose (the specific event: default, refusal, denial, dispossession, etc.), (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — subsequent acts that sustain the right (further demands, further refusals), (3) CONTINUITY — why the cause of action is continuing. Anchor to the actual triggering event from the facts, not to procedural dates like notice.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act 1963 (from enrichment or use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation anchored to the cause of action accrual event, (4) that the suit is within time.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section. State the suit valuation and basis (claim amount for money suits, property value for property suits, etc.). State court fee as per applicable State Court Fees Act or use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder. For property suits, state valuation per Section 7 of the Court Fees Act (market value of share) if applicable.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section IF applicable to this suit type. For money recovery suits: pre-suit interest, pendente lite interest (Section 34 CPC), and future interest with rates and justification. For property suits: mesne profits or compensation if applicable, or state that interest is not the primary relief. Adapt to the actual nature of the suit. If interest is not relevant to this suit type, state briefly that the Plaintiff claims mesne profits / compensation as applicable, or omit interest entirely and instead address any financial relief that fits.

must_include.description: Some form of financial relief or interest stated

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must be specific and tied to the relief sought. Adapt prayers to the actual suit type: for partition suits seek preliminary decree declaring share + final decree for division + separate possession + commissioner appointment; for recovery suits seek decree for principal amount + interest + costs; for injunction suits seek permanent injunction + damages. Always include a general relief clause 'any other relief the Court deems fit'.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon. Map to annexure labels. Adapt documents to the suit type: for property suits include title deeds, genealogy, revenue records, survey sketches; for recovery suits include agreement, payment proof, notice; for family suits include marriage certificate, birth records. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents for this suit type with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. filled_sections.text:
SUIT FOR PARTITION AND SEPARATE POSSESSION OF ANCESTRAL PROPERTY

Between Plaintiff {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}} and Defendant {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} in the District Court of {{DISTRICT}}, {{STATE}}. The Plaintiff seeks partition and separate possession of the ancestral property and a preliminary decree, as the Defendant has refused amicable partition.

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: Between the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, occupation {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, a person sui juris and competent to sue, and the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, occupation {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, this District Court at {{DISTRICT_NAME}} has jurisdiction to entertain this partition suit for ancestral property in which the Plaintiff is entitled to a one-third share, and the Defendant has refused amicable partition. The Plaintiff seeks partition and separate possession of the property described in Schedule of Property at Annexure [SP], and the genealogy illustrating the lineage and coparcenary rights is attached at Annexure [GA]. On or about {{MEETING_DATE}} at {{VILLAGE}}, the Plaintiff made a demand to effect a partition; the Defendant declined to participate. The Plaintiff is filing this suit to obtain a preliminary decree for partition and separate possession.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text:
Territorial jurisdiction: The ancestral property, being immovable, is situated at {{PROPERTY_ADDRESS}}, {{STATE}}. The Plaintiff and Defendant are co-owners, and the Defendant has refused to effect an amicable partition of the ancestral property, which constitutes the cause of action arising from the ongoing co-ownership and continuing as of the filing date. Under Section 16 of the Code of Civil Procedure, jurisdiction to partition immovable property lies in the district where the property is situated; therefore the District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}} is the proper forum for this suit.

Pecuniary jurisdiction: The suit seeks partition and separate possession of an immovable ancestral property and values the subject matter at Rs. {{SUIT_VALUE}}, which is within the pecuniary limits of the District Court of {{COURT_PLACE}} for such suits. The valuation places the action within the court’s monetary jurisdiction to grant a preliminary decree and to proceed with the partition proceedings.

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, and the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, belong to the same ancestral lineage which historically resided at {{PROPERTY_ADDRESS}}. The family genealogy tracing the progenitor and the successive heirs, including the Plaintiff and the Defendant, is annexed as Annexure GT-1 and prepared on {{DATE_OF_GENEALOGY}} to reflect how the line of heirs has descended to the present generation.

2. The ancestral property, as described in the Schedule of Property annexed as Annexure SP-1, comprises immovable property situated at {{PROPERTY_ADDRESS}} and associated rights, along with other ancestral assets listed in the schedule. The annexed schedule sets out the nature of the title and the manner in which the property has been held by the family over generations, with ownership having passed through successive generations to the present claimants.

3. For a period starting from about {{DATE_OF_POSSESSION_START}} the Plaintiff, the Defendant, and other family members held the property in joint possession and enjoyed common use of the land and structures on the property. The family conducted activities such as residence and cultivation, and there was no separate demarcation or exclusive possession of any part by any one member during this period, as described in the family records.

4. On {{DATE_OF_AMICABLE_ATTEMPT}} the Plaintiff proffered an amicable partition by demarcation of shares and possession of the Plaintiff's one-third interest. The parties, including their witnesses, convened at {{PLACE}} to discuss this proposal and the Plaintiff explained the basis for the claim of the Plaintiff's share. The Defendant listened but did not express consent to the proposed demarcation, and the discussion did not result in a final partition.

5. The Defendant subsequently communicated on {{DATE_OF_DEFENDANT_REFUSAL}} a firm refusal to partition the ancestral property and indicated a preference to retain the undivided status of the entire property. The Defendant stated that no portion should be set apart for the Plaintiff at that time and that the property would remain jointly held.

6. In light of the ongoing impasse, the Plaintiff filed a suit in the District Court at {{PLACE}} on {{DATE_OF_FILING}} seeking a preliminary decree for partition and separate possession in respect of the Plaintiff's one-third share in the ancestral property. A copy of the plaint and related documents has been placed on record as Annexure PLAINT-1, and the Schedule of Property and Genealogy annexures are referred to for context as Annexure SP-1 and Annexure GT-1, respectively.

7. The Plaintiff asserts entitlement to a one-third share in the ancestral property described in Annexure SP-1 and seeks a preliminary decree to facilitate the partition and delivery of possession accordingly, subject to further partition as may be ordered by the Court after a suitable commission or measurement if necessary. The relief sought is stated in the accompanying pleadings and is intended to ensure the Plaintiff's peaceful possession without prejudice to final adjustments as may be directed by the Court.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text:
The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, a Hindu governed by Mitakshara law, files this suit in the District Court at {{DISTRICT}} on {{DATE}} for partition and separate possession of ancestral property described in Annexure B and supported by the genealogy in Annexure A, asserting a 1/3rd share in the undivided coparcenary estate and alleging that the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, has refused amicable partition and obstructed the Plaintiff’s right to exclusive possession. The genealogical table in Annexure A establishes the Plaintiff’s status as a coparcener by birth within the family lineage, and Annexure B catalogues the properties that constitute the ancestral estate to be divided. The Plaintiff seeks a preliminary decree directing partition by metes and bounds and allotment of separate possession to the Plaintiff for the 1/3rd share, with liberty to adjust rights and interests in the final decree based on final adjudication of title and possession,, and all necessary ancillary orders to preserve the property during the partition proceedings.

Legal basis and grounds for the suit are anchored in Article 109 of the Limitation Act, which creates a legal right for a Hindu governed by Mitakshara law to set aside his father’s alienation of ancestral property, provided that the action is brought within twelve years from the accrual of cause of action and the accrual occurs when the alienee takes possession. The trigger for this provision occurs where there is an asserted or alleged alienation by the father of ancestral property that would affect the coparcenary rights of the son, enabling the coparcener to challenge such alienation within the prescribed period. In the present case, the Plaintiff, as evidenced by Annexure A, is a coparcener owning 1/3rd share in the ancestral property, and Annexure B identifies the property to be partitioned; thus, if any prior fatherly alienation had occurred that adversely affects the Plaintiff’s coparcenary rights, the limitation period would commence on possession by the alienee and the Plaintiff’s action to preserve or set aside such alienation would be timely under Article 109. Although the present relief is for partition and not exclusively for setting aside an alienation, the existence of potential ancestral alienations remains within the contemplated scope of Article 109, and the Plaintiff’s right to seek partition is not defeated by a mere theoretical barrier to such alienation. The remedy sought here—preliminary partition of the joint ancestral property described in Annexure B—remains consistent with the Plaintiff’s status as coparcener demonstrated in Annexure A, and the relief sought ensures the preservation of the coparcenary interests pending final adjudication.

In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court finds that Article 109 is not applicable to the relief of partition and separate possession, the Plaintiff prays that partition be decreed by metes and bounds and that exclusive possession of the Plaintiff’s 1/3 share be granted, with final adjudication of title and rights in accordance with the evidence adduced at trial, to safeguard the coparcenary interests notwithstanding any limitations argument.

Defensive pleading anticipated: The Plaintiff anticipates that the Defendant may contend that the property is not ancestral or that coparcenary rights do not attach, or that any prior alienation is not within the purview of Article 109; The Plaintiff relies on Annexure A to establish the Mitakshara status and coparcenary entitlement, and on Annexure B to identify the ancestral property subject to partition; The Plaintiff also anticipates that the Defendant may contend time-bar or prejudice due to alienation; The Plaintiff submits that the suit seeks a preliminary decree to prevent waste and to expedite possession, while preserving the rights of all coparceners, and that Article 109 provides a protective mechanism against any attempted alienation that would prejudice 1/3 share; The Plaintiff further contends that the Defendant’s refusal to amicable partition has effectively caused continuing cause of action, justifying the grant of a preliminary decree for partition and separate possession; In addition, the suit seeks to prevent multiplicity of proceedings and preserve the status quo pending final relief, which is a well-founded protective measure in partition actions, particularly where co-owners are in dispute.

Annexures: Genealogy Table at Annexure A; Schedule of Property at Annexure B.

filled_sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
filled_sections.text: Origin: On {{DATE_OF_TRIGGERING_EVENT}} at place_names, the right to sue for partition and separate possession first arose when the Defendant_name refused to entertain an amicable partition of the ancestral property described in Schedule of Property at property_address, which is jointly owned by Plaintiff_name and Defendant_name as ancestral property, with the Plaintiff_name entitled to a one-third share as evidenced by the genealogical table in Annexure GA. Accrual: From that date onward, the Defendant_name has repeatedly refused to participate in any partition negotiation and has obstructed the realization of the Plaintiff_name’s one-third share by maintain­ing the property undivided, thereby sustaining and enlarging the Plaintiff_name’s claim to partition and possession and making a schedule of possession, as contemplated in the relief sought, necessary. Continuity: The cause remains continuing because the property described in Schedule of Property at property_address continues to be undivided, the Defendant_name’s ongoing refusal to partition prevents the Plaintiff_name from obtaining possession of their rightful one-third share, and the Plaintiff_name seeks a preliminary decree to effect partition and delivery of possession, as supported by the genealogical table attached at Annexure GA and the Schedule of Property attached at Annexure SB. The Plaintiff_name states the entitlement and seeks the court’s intervention to determine and grant the partition and possession in accordance with law.

filled_sections.heading: LIMITATION
filled_sections.text: The limitation governing this partition suit is governed by Article 109 of the Limitation Act, 1963, which prescribes a twelve-year period for a Hindu governed by Mitakshara law to set aside his father’s alienation of ancestral property. The limitation period is twelve years. The accrual occurs on the date when possession of the ancestral property is taken by the alienee, i.e., on {{ACCRUAL_DATE}}. The Plaintiff filed this suit before the District Court on {{FILING_DATE}}, which is within twelve years from the accrual date; accordingly, the suit is within time.

filled_sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
filled_sections.text: Valuation and Court Fee: The Plaintiff seeks partition and separate possession of the ancestral property comprising the Plaintiff's one-third share, the property being described in Schedule of Property and situated at {{PROPERTY_LOCATION}}. For purposes of court fee, valuation is based on the market value of the Plaintiff's share in the property, computed in accordance with Section 7 of the Court Fees Act. The market value of the Plaintiff's one-third share is Rs {{MARKET_VALUE_OF_SHARE}}. The court fee payable on the plaint is Rs {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} under the applicable State Court Fees Act.

filled_sections.heading: INTEREST
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, is entitled to a 1/3rd share in the ancestral property described in Schedule of Property annexed to this plaint and seeks partition and separate possession. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, has refused amicable partition and continues in possession of the entire property, thereby depriving the Plaintiff of his/her rightful share. The Plaintiff claims mesne profits for the period from {{INSTITUTION_DATE}} up to the date of decree or delivery of possession, with reference to the prevailing market rent and calculated proportionately for the Plaintiff’s 1/3 interest; the quantum shall be subject to adjustment in the final partition. Mesne profits are requested at the rate of {{MESNE_RATE}} per annum, to be computed on a pro rata basis for the Plaintiff’s share. The Plaintiff prays for a preliminary decree for partition and separate possession after measurement and valuation by a Commissioner and for inclusion of mesne profits in the final decree, along with such further relief as may be just and proper. Since this is a property suit, pre-suit and pendente lite interest are not claimed; instead the Plaintiff seeks compensation in the form of mesne profits for wrongful possession and the grant of partition so that possession can be delivered to the Plaintiff for his/her share. The property is described in Schedule of Property annexed, and the Plaintiff requests that the relevant Schedule and Commissioner’s report be annexed to the final decree.

filled_sections.heading: PRAYER
LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
filled_sections.text:
1. Annexure A: Copy of the original title deeds and mutation/Record of Rights of the ancestral property, evidencing the Plaintiff's one-third share; the Plaintiff relies on these documents to establish title and co-ownership, the deeds having been registered on {{TITLE_DEED_REGISTRATION_DATE}} at {{TITLE_DEED_REGISTRATION_PLACE}} and presently on record with the relevant land registry. 

2. Annexure B: Genealogy table tracing the Plaintiff's lineage from the common ancestor to the Plaintiff and Defendant, prepared for the partition suit on {{GENEALOGY_DATE}} at {{GENEALOGY_PLACE}} to corroborate the Plaintiff's entitlement to a share in the property. 

3. Annexure C: Revenue records for the suit property, including latest Extracts/Record of Rights indicating possession and description, obtained from the office of the Revenue Department on {{REVENUE_RECORD_DATE}} at {{REVENUE_RECORD_PLACE}}; these records support the claim of ancestral co-ownership. 

4. Annexure D: Survey sketch/plan of the ancestral property delineating boundaries and proposed demarcation lines; prepared by a licensed surveyor on {{SURVEY_DATE}} at {{SURVEY_PLACE}} and annexed to map the property for partition. 

5. Annexure E: Schedule of Property listing the parcels, survey numbers, descriptions, and the Plaintiff's share; drafted on {{SCHEDULE_DATE}} at {{SCHEDULE_PLACE}}; annexed to define the scope of partition.

IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

PLAINT UNDER THE CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE, 1908
SUIT FOR PARTITION, SEPARATE POSSESSION AND FOR PRELIMINARY DECREE


BETWEEN:

Plaintiff:
{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, occupation {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, a person sui juris and competent to sue.


AND

Defendant(s):
1. {{DEFENDANT1_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT1_AGE}} years, occupation {{DEFENDANT1_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT1_ADDRESS}}.

2. {{DEFENDANT2_NAME}} (if any other co-sharer/legal heir/co-owner is required to be impleaded), aged {{DEFENDANT2_AGE}} years, occupation {{DEFENDANT2_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT2_ADDRESS}}.

(Collectively referred to as “Defendants”, as applicable.)


MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

1. The Plaintiff and the Defendants are members/legal heirs/co-owners of the family of late {{PROPOSITUS_NAME}} (the common ancestor/propositus), and claim rights in the suit properties described in the SCHEDULE OF PROPERTY appended hereto (Annexure B).

2. The genealogy of the family showing the relationship between the parties and the line of succession is set out in ANNEXURE A (Genealogy Table) to this plaint.

3. The suit properties described in the SCHEDULE OF PROPERTY (Annexure B) are stated to be ancestral/joint family properties of the said family and are presently undivided. The Plaintiff states that no partition by metes and bounds has taken place in respect of the suit properties, and the Plaintiff continues to be entitled to seek partition and separate possession.

4. The Plaintiff states that he/she is entitled to a 1/3rd undivided share in the suit properties. The Defendants are entitled to the remaining shares as may be determined by this Hon’ble Court upon consideration of the genealogy and the applicable law.

5. The Plaintiff states that the parties have been in joint possession/enjoyment of the suit properties. However, disputes have arisen, and the Plaintiff has been seeking an amicable partition.

6. On {{DATE_OF_AMICABLE_ATTEMPT}} at {{PLACE_OF_MEETING}}, the Plaintiff demanded partition and separate possession of the Plaintiff’s 1/3rd share. The Defendant No.1 refused/declined to effect an amicable partition on {{DATE_OF_DEFENDANT_REFUSAL}} (oral/written refusal, as applicable).

7. The Plaintiff states that due to the refusal and continuing denial of partition, it has become necessary for the Plaintiff to approach this Hon’ble Court seeking a preliminary decree declaring the shares of the parties and for further proceedings leading to a final decree for partition by metes and bounds.


JURISDICTION

8. Territorial Jurisdiction: The suit properties are immovable properties situated at {{PROPERTY_ADDRESS}}, within the territorial jurisdiction of this Hon’ble Court. Under Section 16 of the Code of Civil Procedure, jurisdiction to entertain a suit for partition of immovable property lies in the Court within whose jurisdiction the property is situated.

9. Pecuniary Jurisdiction: For the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee, the suit is valued at Rs. {{SUIT_VALUE}} (subject to verification as per the applicable State law), which is within the pecuniary limits of this Hon’ble Court.


FACTS OF THE CASE (CHRONOLOGICAL)

10. Late {{PROPOSITUS_NAME}} was the common ancestor/propositus of the parties. The Plaintiff sets out the family lineage in Annexure A.

11. The suit properties described in Annexure B are the joint/ancestral properties claimed to form part of the undivided family estate. The Plaintiff relies upon the revenue records/title documents and other materials described in the List of Documents.

12. The Plaintiff and the Defendants, being co-owners/co-sharers, have rights in every inch of the suit properties until partition. The Plaintiff has been requesting division and separate possession of the Plaintiff’s 1/3rd share.

13. Despite demands made on {{DATE_OF_AMICABLE_ATTEMPT}} and thereafter, the Defendant No.1 has refused partition on {{DATE_OF_DEFENDANT_REFUSAL}} and has failed to cooperate in effecting an amicable partition.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

14. The Plaintiff is entitled to seek partition and separate possession of the Plaintiff’s share in the undivided suit properties. A partition suit proceeds in stages, including passing of a preliminary decree declaring the shares of the parties and thereafter a final decree effecting division by metes and bounds.

15. The Plaintiff seeks a preliminary decree declaring the Plaintiff’s 1/3rd share and declaring/ascertaining the shares of the Defendants as well, followed by appointment of a Commissioner to effect partition and to submit a report/plan for final decree proceedings.


CAUSE OF ACTION

16. The cause of action first arose on {{DATE_OF_AMICABLE_ATTEMPT}} at {{PLACE_OF_MEETING}} when the Plaintiff demanded partition and separate possession and the Defendant No.1 did not agree.

17. The cause of action further arose on {{DATE_OF_DEFENDANT_REFUSAL}} when the Defendant No.1 expressly refused to effect an amicable partition.

18. The cause of action is continuing from the said dates and continues to subsist on the date of filing, as the suit properties remain undivided and the Plaintiff continues to be denied separate possession of the Plaintiff’s asserted 1/3rd share.


LIMITATION

19. The Plaintiff states that the present suit for partition is within limitation, the right to sue having arisen upon refusal/denial of partition as pleaded above, and the cause of action being continuing so long as the suit properties remain undivided and the Plaintiff’s demand for partition is denied.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

20. For the purposes of court fee and jurisdiction, the suit is valued at Rs. {{SUIT_VALUE}} and the Plaintiff’s 1/3rd share is valued at Rs. {{MARKET_VALUE_OF_SHARE}} (as required under the applicable State Court Fees law).

21. The requisite court fee of Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} is paid (or shall be paid) in accordance with the applicable Court Fees Act / State amendment as in force in {{STATE}}.


MESNE PROFITS / PROFITS (IF CLAIMED)

22. The Plaintiff states that the question of profits/mesne profits from the suit properties (if any) may be inquired into at the stage of final decree proceedings and prays for appropriate directions accordingly.


PRAYER

WHEREFORE, the Plaintiff prays that this Hon’ble Court be pleased to:

(a) Pass a PRELIMINARY DECREE declaring that the Plaintiff is entitled to a 1/3rd undivided share in the suit properties described in the SCHEDULE OF PROPERTY (Annexure B) and declaring/ascertaining the shares of the Defendants and all other necessary co-sharers, if any;

(b) Direct partition of the suit properties by metes and bounds and appoint a Commissioner for effecting partition and for preparation of a plan/schedule of allotment for final decree proceedings;

(c) Upon receipt of the Commissioner’s report and after necessary inquiry, pass a FINAL DECREE for partition and separate possession by allotting to the Plaintiff the Plaintiff’s 1/3rd share (or such share as determined) and for delivery of vacant and peaceful possession thereof;

(d) Grant costs of the suit and final decree proceedings; and

(e) Grant such other and further reliefs as this Hon’ble Court may deem fit in the facts and circumstances of the case.


LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

1. Annexure A: Genealogy Table of the family of late {{PROPOSITUS_NAME}}.
2. Annexure B: Schedule of Property (suit properties).
3. Annexure C: Copies of title documents (if available) / partition-related family documents (if any) / link documents pertaining to the suit properties. ({{TITLE_DOC_DETAILS}})
4. Annexure D: Revenue records / RTC / Jamabandi / Khata / Mutation Extracts / Property tax receipts relating to the suit properties. ({{REVENUE_RECORD_DETAILS}})
5. Annexure E: Copy of demand/notice for partition dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and proof of service / reply, if any. ({{NOTICE_DETAILS}})


VERIFICATION

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to {{LAST_FACT_PARAGRAPH_NO}} are true and correct to my knowledge and belief, and that I have not suppressed any material fact.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.


Through:
{{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
Advocate
Enrollment No.: {{ADVOCATE_ENROLLMENT}}
Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}
Date: {{FILING_DATE}}

citations_used: Order VI Rule 15, Code of Civil Procedure, 1908

blocking_issues.issue: Statutory reference cited without RAG support — may be sourced from training memory which can produce incorrect provision numbers for this cause of action type
blocking_issues.fix: Remove the specific citation to Article 109 of the Limitation Act (or replace with a non-numbered reference such as “as per the applicable provisions of the Limitation Act, 1963”); align the limitation discussion to a partition suit (right to sue triggered by denial/refusal/ouster, and otherwise continuing cause of action). Verify the correct article before filing if you intend to cite a specific one.

blocking_issues.issue: Statutory reference cited without RAG support — may be sourced from training memory which can produce incorrect provision numbers for this cause of action type
blocking_issues.fix: Remove the specific citation to “Section 7 of the Court Fees Act” (or replace with “as per the applicable Court Fees Act / State amendment” without a section number). If a specific provision must be cited, verify it from the applicable State Court Fees enactment before filing.

blocking_issues.issue: Cause of action / limitation is based on an inapplicable accrual event (alienee taking possession under Article 109) rather than the accrual relevant to a partition claim (denial/refusal/ouster) and/or the continuing nature of joint possession
blocking_issues.fix: Rewrite the Limitation paragraph to anchor timeliness to the denial/refusal of partition ({{DATE_OF_DEFENDANT_REFUSAL}}) and/or plead continuing cause of action due to continued joint status and ongoing denial; remove references to alienee possession unless the suit actually challenges a specific alienation and includes that relief.

blocking_issues.issue: Mandatory content requested and necessary for filing is missing in-body: genealogy table and Schedule of Property are only referenced as annexures without being set out (and the schedule lacks property particulars)
blocking_issues.fix: Insert (a) a Genealogy Table (with names/relationships/deaths if applicable) and (b) a Schedule of Property containing full identification particulars (survey numbers/khata/khasra, extent, boundaries, municipal details, etc.) in the plaint text (or as annexures reproduced in the filing set).
blocking_issues.location: Genealogy references throughout; Schedule of Property references throughout; end of plaint

blocking_issues.issue: Document reference label mismatch: multiple inconsistent annexure schemes used (e.g., Annexure [SP]/[GA], GT-1/SP-1, Annexure A/B, Annexure I/II, and List of Documents Annexure A–E), creating filing inconsistency
blocking_issues.fix: Adopt one consistent annexure/label scheme across the entire plaint and the List of Documents (e.g., Annexure A = Genealogy; Annexure B = Schedule of Property; Annexure C = Title/Revenue records; etc.), and update all internal cross-references to match exactly.
blocking_issues.location: Introductory paragraphs; FACTS; LEGAL BASIS; CAUSE OF ACTION; PRAYER; LIST OF DOCUMENTS

blocking_issues.issue: Plaint contains untemplated variable tokens (e.g., “place_names”, “Defendant_name”, “Plaintiff_name”, “property_address”) which are not valid pleading particulars and may lead to filing defects if left as-is
blocking_issues.fix: Convert all such tokens to {{PLACEHOLDER}} format or replace with actual particulars; ensure consistency with earlier placeholders (e.g., use {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, {{DEFENDANT1_NAME}}, {{PROPERTY_ADDRESS}}).

non_blocking_issues.issue: Legal analysis is mixed into the facts/jurisdiction narrative (e.g., statutory references and legal conclusions embedded in factual paragraphs)
non_blocking_issues.fix: Move legal propositions and statutory references into the “Legal Basis and Grounds” section; keep the “Facts” section strictly chronological factual events.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Jurisdiction pleading lacks specific territorial connecting facts beyond property situs (e.g., where parties reside/carry on business, where refusal occurred, where documents/records are maintained)
non_blocking_issues.fix: Add specific territorial links (Defendants’ residence within district; meeting/refusal location; revenue office location; property municipal limits) to strengthen jurisdiction averments.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Prayer could more clearly distinguish preliminary decree relief from final decree proceedings and include a specific prayer for mesne profits / inquiry (if intended)
non_blocking_issues.fix: Split prayer into: (i) preliminary decree declaring shares of all parties; (ii) appointment of commissioner; (iii) inquiry into mesne profits (if claimed) to be determined in final decree; (iv) final decree after commissioner report; (v) costs and other relief.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Facts are largely template-level and may be viewed as conclusory unless supported by concrete particulars (property identifiers, dates of death, prior partition denial details, possession/enjoyment specifics)
non_blocking_issues.fix: Add specific dates/events: death of propositus, succession events, when and how joint possession/enjoyment occurred, exact refusal words/notice, and any acts of exclusion/ouster if any.

unsupported_statements.statement: “The Plaintiff… a Hindu governed by Mitakshara law … status as a coparcener by birth …”
unsupported_statements.reason: User request states “ancestral property” but does not provide the parties’ personal law/religion or whether the property is coparcenary/joint family property versus inherited/tenancy-in-common; this must be confirmed and pleaded accurately.

unsupported_statements.statement: “Legal basis and grounds … anchored in Article 109 of the Limitation Act …” and “accrual occurs when the alienee takes possession”
unsupported_statements.reason: No facts are pleaded about any father’s alienation, any alienee, or alienee possession; the limitation theory is unsupported by the pleaded factual matrix and mismatched to a pure partition claim.

unsupported_statements.statement: “Copy of the plaint … has been placed on record as Annexure PLAINT-1”
unsupported_statements.reason: A plaint is the pleading being filed, not typically an annexure to itself; statement appears template-like and not grounded in procedural practice for a fresh filing.
